A Fair and Impartial Inquiry
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Recent criticism of Kenneth W. Starr, the Whitewater independent counsel, for purported conflicts of interest in his representation of private clients is fundamentally wrong. It ignores the mandates of the independent counsel law, the realities of any investigative process and Mr. Starr's own role as aspecial prosecutor and private lawyer. Perhaps most significantly, it fails to appreciate Mr. Starr himself. 

     In writing the independent counsel statute, Congress provided that the prosecutors chosen should not become full-time Federal employees. Of the 17 independent counsels, 16 have continued their private practice.

COMPANY BRIEFS
Arts Backed as Aid For Troubled Youths
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In a high-level plug for community arts programs for young people, a White House panel has prepared a report documenting how such programs can foster creative enrichment and academic improvement.  The report, "Coming Up Taller," by the President's Committee on the Arts and the Huma'ities, says that "safe havens" of music, theater, dance and visual arts programs have proved "particularly potent" in stemming violence and drug abuse and in keeping students from dropping out of school. It is to be ceremonially released this afternoon at the White House by Hillary Rodham Clinton, the actor Richard Dreyfuss and Jane Alexander, chairwoman of the National Endowment for the Arts,  among others. 

     The committee reviewed 218 programs in 36 states and the District of Columbia, including a California program, "Sentenced to the Stage," in which juvenile offenders in the Los Angeles area joined drama and dance workshops as a condition of their probation, and a Manhattan media workshop, the Educational Video Center, where high school students shoot programs for their own show, "YO-TV." Not all programs in the country made the report.

The Last Battle For Troy
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Moscow's Pushkin Museum has restored to world view, for the first time in more than 50 years, the ancient and fabled artifacts known as the Trojan Gold. But the artistic importance and romantic history of the treasure were overshadowed at the exhibit's opening when Russian and German officials squabbled over the custody of the trove, which was removed from Berlin by Soviet troops in 1945. 

     The Germans are technically correct in asserting ownership of the treasure. But political disputes over the artifacts of ancient cultures can be even less clear-cut than conflicts over borders or missiles. Germany should consider allowing the gold to stay permanently on view at the Pushkin. This could pave the way for Russia's return of objects far more essential to German culture.

wiki_events.headline: Whitewater controversy: President Bill Clinton gives a 41⁄2 hour videotaped testimony for the defense.

pages.title: Whitewater controversy
pages.abstract: The Whitewater controversy, Whitewater scandal, or simply Whitewater, was an American political controversy during the 1990s, surrounding the Whitewater Development Corporation, a real estate company owned by President Bill Clinton and First Lady Hillary Clinton and their associates, Jim and Susan McDougal.

pages.title: Bill Clinton
pages.description: President of the United States from 1993 to 2001
pages.abstract: William Jefferson Clinton is an American politician and lawyer who served as the 42nd president of the United States from 1993 to 2001. A member of the Democratic Party, he previously served as the attorney general of Arkansas from 1977 to 1979 and as the governor of Arkansas from 1979 to 1981, and again from 1983 to 1992. His centrist "Third Way" political philosophy became known as Clintonism, which dominated his presidency and the succeeding decades of Democratic Party history.

wiki_events.headline: Port Arthur massacre, Tasmania: A gunman, Martin Bryant, opens fire at the Broad Arrow Cafe in Port Arthur, Tasmania, killing 35 people and wounding 23 others.

pages.title: Port Arthur massacre
pages.abstract: The Port Arthur massacre was a mass shooting that occurred on 28 April 1996 at Port Arthur, a tourist town in the Australian state of Tasmania. The perpetrator, Martin Bryant, killed 35 people and wounded 23 others, in the deadliest massacre in modern Australian history. The attack led to fundamental changes in Australia's gun laws.

pages.title: Martin Bryant
pages.abstract: Martin John Bryant is an Australian mass murderer who shot and killed 35 people and injured 23 others in the Port Arthur massacre on 28 and 29 April 1996. He is currently serving 35 life sentences, and 1,652 years without the possibility of parole, at Risdon Prison in Hobart, Tasmania.

pages.title: Port Arthur, Tasmania
pages.description: UNESCO World Heritage Site in Australia
pages.abstract: Port Arthur is a town and former convict settlement on the Tasman Peninsula, in Tasmania, Australia. It is located approximately 97 kilometres (60 mi) southeast of the state capital, Hobart.
